startScreenOptions.title: PY2.1-PY2.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY2.1-PY2.9.

interactions.label: A blood sample is allowed to clot and the liquid portion is collected. What is t

params.question: A blood sample is allowed to clot and the liquid portion is collected. What is this fluid called, and why?

answers.text: Plasma — it is the cell-free portion of anticoagulated blood

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Plasma is obtained when clotting is prevented by an anticoagulant. It still contains fibrinogen.

answers.text: Serum — it is plasma minus fibrinogen, the fluid remaining after clot formation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. When blood clots, fibrinogen is consumed to form the fibrin mesh, leaving serum behind.

answers.text: Buffy coat — it is the pale layer that separates after centrifugation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The buffy coat is a thin layer of white blood cells and platelets sitting between red cells and plasma after centrifugation. It is not a fluid.

answers.text: Transudate — it is the protein-poor filtrate that seeps from clotted blood

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Transudate refers to fluid filtered across capillary walls due to hydrostatic or oncotic pressure imbalances, not to a product of clotted blood.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: when blood clots, fibrinogen is used up to form fibrin. The remaining fluid is serum. Plasma retains fibrinogen because clotting is prevented.
Exactly right. Serum = plasma − fibrinogen. This distinction matters in the lab: serum tubes have no anticoagulant, while plasma tubes do.

interactions.label: In a healthy adult, the liver and spleen are the primary sites of red blood cell

params.question: In a healthy adult, the liver and spleen are the primary sites of red blood cell production.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is false. The liver and spleen produce blood cells during fetal development, but after birth, the bone marrow takes over. In healthy adults, haematopoiesis occurs in the red marrow of flat bones (sternum, ribs, iliac crest, vertebrae).
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the liver and spleen are major haematopoietic organs only during fetal life (second trimester). In adults, red blood cell production occurs in red bone marrow of flat bones such as the sternum, ribs, iliac crest, and vertebrae.

metadata.title: In a healthy adult, the liver and spleen

interactions.label: During vigorous exercise, skeletal muscle becomes hot, acidic, and rich in CO₂.

params.question: During vigorous exercise, skeletal muscle becomes hot, acidic, and rich in CO₂. What effect does this have on the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve?

answers.text: Left shift — haemoglobin binds oxygen more tightly, increasing delivery to muscle

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A left shift means tighter oxygen binding, which would reduce delivery to tissues — the opposite of what exercising muscle needs.

answers.text: Right shift — haemoglobin releases oxygen more readily, increasing delivery to muscle

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Rising temperature, falling pH, and rising CO₂ all shift the curve right (the Bohr effect), enhancing oxygen unloading precisely where demand is highest.

answers.text: No shift — the curve is fixed and does not respond to local tissue conditions

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The curve is not fixed. Multiple physiological factors shift it left or right, allowing haemoglobin to fine-tune oxygen delivery to changing tissue demands.

answers.text: Right shift — haemoglobin binds oxygen more tightly, reducing delivery to muscle

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A right shift does occur, but it reduces oxygen affinity, meaning haemoglobin releases oxygen more readily — not more tightly.

overallFeedback.feedback: The Bohr effect: rising CO₂ and falling pH shift the curve right, making haemoglobin release oxygen more easily. This is why exercising muscles get preferential oxygen delivery.
Well done. The Bohr effect ensures that active tissues with high CO₂ and low pH receive more oxygen — an elegant match between supply and demand.

interactions.label: A patient's complete blood count shows a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 110 fL

params.question: A patient's complete blood count shows a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 110 fL. Which type of anaemia does this indicate, and which is the most likely cause?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Iron deficiency causes microcytic anaemia (MCV < 80 fL), not a raised MCV of 110 fL.

answers.text: Normocytic anaemia — most likely chronic kidney disease

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Chronic kidney disease typically causes normocytic anaemia (MCV 80–100 fL). An MCV of 110 fL is well above the normal range.

answers.text: Macrocytic anaemia — most likely vitamin B₁₂ or folate deficiency

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. An MCV above 100 fL defines macrocytic anaemia. Megaloblastic anaemia from B₁₂ or folate deficiency is the most important cause, as impaired DNA synthesis leads to abnormally large red cells.
Iron deficiency produces small (microcytic) red cells, not large ones. An MCV of 110 fL points to macrocytic causes such as B₁₂ or folate deficiency.

overallFeedback.feedback: An MCV of 110 fL is macrocytic (>100 fL). The most important cause is megaloblastic anaemia from vitamin B₁₂ or folate deficiency. Iron deficiency causes microcytic anaemia (<80 fL), and CKD causes normocytic anaemia (80–100 fL).
Exactly. MCV > 100 fL = macrocytic. Think megaloblastic anaemia from B₁₂/folate deficiency first — impaired DNA replication prevents normal cell division, producing oversized red cells.

interactions.label: Low-dose aspirin prevents heart attacks by irreversibly inhibiting cyclooxygenas

params.question: Low-dose aspirin prevents heart attacks by irreversibly inhibiting cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A₂ production for the platelet's entire lifespan.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Close, but the enzyme is wrong. Aspirin irreversibly blocks COX-1, not COX-2, in platelets. COX-1 is the constitutive isoform responsible for thromboxane A₂ synthesis. Since platelets have no nucleus, they cannot make new COX-1, so the effect lasts the platelet's entire 8–12 day lifespan.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — aspirin's antiplatelet effect works through COX-1, not COX-2. COX-1 is the constitutive enzyme in platelets that produces thromboxane A₂. Because platelets lack a nucleus and cannot synthesise new enzyme, the inhibition lasts for the platelet's full 8–12 day lifespan.

interactions.label: An Rh-negative mother delivers her first Rh-positive baby without receiving anti

params.question: An Rh-negative mother delivers her first Rh-positive baby without receiving anti-D immunoglobulin. In which pregnancy is haemolytic disease of the newborn most likely to occur, and why?

answers.text: The first pregnancy — maternal IgM antibodies cross the placenta immediately

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: IgM antibodies are too large to cross the placenta. Moreover, significant anti-D antibodies typically develop after delivery of the first Rh-positive baby, not during the first pregnancy itself.

answers.text: The second Rh-positive pregnancy — maternal IgG anti-D antibodies from the first sensitisation cross the placenta and attack fetal red cells

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Sensitisation occurs when fetal Rh-positive cells enter the mother's circulation at first delivery. In a subsequent Rh-positive pregnancy, memory B cells mount a rapid IgG response that crosses the placenta and haemolyses fetal red cells.

answers.text: The second Rh-positive pregnancy — maternal IgM anti-D antibodies accumulate and cross the placenta

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The antibody class matters. IgG, not IgM, crosses the placenta. The secondary immune response produces IgG anti-D, which is small enough to cross the placental barrier.

answers.text: Any pregnancy — Rh antibodies are naturally occurring like ABO antibodies

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Unlike ABO antibodies, Rh antibodies are not naturally occurring. They develop only after exposure to Rh-positive red cells through transfusion or pregnancy. This is why the first pregnancy usually goes unaffected.

overallFeedback.feedback: Sensitisation happens at first delivery when fetal Rh-positive cells enter the mother's blood. Her immune system makes anti-D IgG antibodies. In the next Rh-positive pregnancy, these IgG antibodies cross the placenta and destroy fetal red cells. Prevention: anti-D immunoglobulin at 28 weeks and within 72 hours postpartum.

Well done. This is why anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) is given at 28 weeks and within 72 hours of delivery — it destroys fetal Rh-positive cells before the mother's immune system can mount a response.
